narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 42—well above the typical 37—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 52% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, tipping the decision toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of students' academic paths, family backgrounds, and bar outcomes, focusing on who tends to pass or struggle. The model's call hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—far below the average of about 5.5—emerging as the single biggest driver pushing toward a failure prediction, as it signals a notable dip in performance compared to peers. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.4, lower than the typical 3.2, added to that concern as the second-most influential factor, reinforcing worries about sustaining strong grades through law school and beyond. On a brighter note, your strong LSAT stood out as the top positive influence overall, pulling the prediction back toward success more than any other element in your profile. While your middle family income bracket and full-time student status align with most applicants—over 90% of whom go full-time—they played smaller roles here. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your later-year grades lag behind those who rank higher in their deciles and maintain stronger GPAs from undergrad, even if your LSAT gives you an edge many don't have. This close 52% call means you're right on the bubble, not a clear outlier. To turn things around, consider retaking courses or targeted prep to boost your recent academic decile, perhaps through community college refreshers or tutoring, and aim to lift that GPA with post-baccalaureate work. Retaking the LSAT isn't urgent given your score, but building a narrative of upward momentum in applications to other schools could sway human reviewers. Many students in similar spots succeed by addressing these gaps head-on—your potential is there, especially with that LSAT strength. Keep pushing; reapplying with improvements often flips the script.
